caveats: a: No source directly measures a wage-weighted AI-exposed task share for NAICS 332996 or the narrowed lens.
a: The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.3209 combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and includes commodity producers and other business models outside the lens.
rho: The welding case demonstrates adjacent automation feasibility, not AI implementation across the target population.
rho: Implementation differs materially between repetitive OEM tube assemblies and one-off coded process-pipe spools.
e: No source measures the qualifying custom-fabricator share of LMM firms in this six-digit code.
e: The provided firm count of 217 is estimated from size-class receipts and a margin bridge; multi-establishment parents and project timing can distort the LMM mapping.
s5: The transactions span adjacent tube fabrication and process-skid activities, so exact six-digit classification may vary.
s5: Published examples are not a population hazard rate and omit failed processes and internal family transfers.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-enabled gross benefit for the narrowed lens.
q: OEM releases, municipal project bids, process skids, and emergency industrial work have different pricing power and sharing mechanisms.
d5: Water infrastructure is only one end market and may not represent the code's full customer mix.
d5: Public-company revenue and backlog combine price, steel cost, product mix, and project timing rather than pure constant-quality quantity.
o: This is bounded judgment, not an observed displacement rate.
o: Operator requirement can persist while work shifts to a captive shop or a different fabrication technology.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers issue recurring purchase orders, releases, and project packages for made-to-spec components and assemblies. Revenue is quoted per piece, spool, assembly, lot, or project, often on a fixed-price basis, with engineering review, material procurement, tooling, welding, inspection, testing, documentation, and delivery embedded or separately charged.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes commodity product manufacturing with custom job, project, and repeat OEM fabrication. The lens is narrowed to custom and build-to-print operators whose recurring external-customer fabrication revenue forms a coherent outsourced service; the narrowing is for business-model consistency, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Commodity catalog pipe or fitting brands without meaningful custom outsourced-fabrication revenue; primary pipe mills; distributors; field-only plumbing, mechanical, pipeline, and installation contractors; captive fabrication shops; plastic pipe production outside the code; and firms outside the lower-middle-market control-acquisition band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market custom and build-to-print fabricators that repeatedly bend, cut, weld, flange, machine, assemble, inspect, test, finish, or package pipe, tube, fittings, spools, metering runs, hydraulic assemblies, and related skids for external OEM, industrial, water, energy, construction, and infrastructure customers.

a.bridge: NIST documents AI use cases for small and medium manufacturers generally, while pipe and tube fabricators document quoting, engineering, CNC bending, welding, inspection, testing, and production workflows. I map predictive maintenance and quality, demand and material forecasting, nesting and schedule optimization, document extraction, estimate support, and inspection analytics to the lens's wage mix. Existing CNC and robotic welding are treated as already automated and excluded, which may make the estimate conservative for shops yet to adopt them but prevents counting conventional automation as AI.
caveats: No source directly measures a wage-weighted AI-exposed task share for NAICS 332996 or the narrowed lens.
The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.3209 combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and includes commodity producers and other business models outside the lens.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupation roster and task-level time study from eligible fabrication shops, with explicit separation of current CNC or robotic automation from not-yet-automated AI-addressable work, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI exposure is concentrated in drawing and bill-of-material extraction, estimating, design-for-manufacture review, scheduling, material optimization, machine-health analysis, quality and weld-data review, documentation, purchasing, and customer administration. Fitting, setup, material handling, welding, finishing, inspection, and test execution remain substantially physical.

d5.bridge: EPA measures long-term US drinking-water infrastructure need and NWPX reports healthy near- and medium-term water-project demand, but neither measures constant-price year-five demand for the full custom pipe and fitting lens. I combine water replacement support with the lens's broader OEM, industrial, energy, and construction exposure and moderate the outlook for project timing, housing, rates, and funding delays. Water evidence may bias the base high for energy-facing shops and low for fabricators serving data-center, semiconductor, or other faster-growth projects.
caveats: Water infrastructure is only one end market and may not represent the code's full customer mix.
Public-company revenue and backlog combine price, steel cost, product mix, and project timing rather than pure constant-quality quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price fabricated-product shipments by end market, eligible-firm backlog and utilization, EPA project-award timing, OEM production forecasts, and industrial construction starts would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Aging water systems, funded infrastructure programs, urban growth, industrial maintenance, and repeat OEM equipment production support modest real demand growth. Construction cycles, interest rates, project delays, steel volatility, energy transitions, customer insourcing, and regional exposure create credible near-flat downside.

caveats: No source measures the qualifying custom-fabricator share of LMM firms in this six-digit code.
The provided firm count of 217 is estimated from size-class receipts and a margin bridge; multi-establishment parents and project timing can distort the LMM mapping.
e.change_evidence: A parent-level census using Census or Data Axle records, websites, product catalogs, customer and project disclosures, field-versus-shop revenue, and verified EBITDA bands would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: Custom OEM tube and turnkey pipe-fabrication examples demonstrate a substantial outsourced-service population, and the code's LMM firms are more likely than large commodity plants to compete on repeat custom work. Eligibility is reduced by catalog manufacturers, captive shops, field contractors, distributors, misclassified machine shops, and firms without sufficient normalized earnings.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332996 ($2,444,251,461) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332996 ($8,120,497,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $305,110 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332996); class <5: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $524,355 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $102,878 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 104 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,824,519 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $357,971 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,523,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $691,278 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 153 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,047,118 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,363,644 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,457,141 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,779,491 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $73,853,157 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,489,989 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 217

caveats: This is bounded judgment, not an observed displacement rate.
Operator requirement can persist while work shifts to a captive shop or a different fabrication technology.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and capital plans covering make-versus-buy, modularization, additive manufacturing, joint technologies, prefabrication, supplier consolidation, and insourcing would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five demand still requires physical pipe, fittings, bends, spools, welds, assemblies, inspection, testing, and delivery. Software can design, coordinate, and partially automate production but cannot supply the material product or accountable pressure-boundary manufacturer. Some customers may insource, use modular alternatives, or redesign assemblies, keeping the base below one.

q.bridge: NWPX is a larger engineered water-pipe producer rather than an LMM custom fabricator, but it reports fixed-price quoting, steel-cost exposure, bidding, and project timing. I translate fixed-price and per-project structures to initial savings retention, then discount for competitive rebids, customer cost knowledge, annual OEM negotiations, and eventual repricing. This may understate retention for low-competition qualified specialty work and overstate it where open-book or cost-plus terms apply.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-enabled gross benefit for the narrowed lens.
OEM releases, municipal project bids, process skids, and emergency industrial work have different pricing power and sharing mechanisms.
q.change_evidence: Customer MSAs, quote and renewal histories, steel-escalation and open-book clauses, rebid cadence, OEM annual price-downs, win-loss data, and gross-margin bridges after productivity investments would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed-price quotes allow the operator to retain unanticipated labor, scrap, scheduling, and yield gains within a job or contract. Over five years, customers rebid repeat work, benchmark labor and material content, demand price-downs, and share value through renewal negotiations; reliable delivery, engineering help, quality, and scarce certifications protect part of the gain.

rho.bridge: NIST's implementation guidance applies to small and medium manufacturers broadly, and the pipe-spool welding case shows successful shop-floor automation rather than an AI implementation rate. I translate proven digitizable workflows, quality data, and incremental pilot economics into the implemented share of exposed AI opportunity, then discount for fragmented drawings, high product mix, code compliance, customer approval, data scarcity, and change management. The proxy may be high for small manual shops and low for larger digital fabricators with repeat families of parts.
caveats: The welding case demonstrates adjacent automation feasibility, not AI implementation across the target population.
Implementation differs materially between repetitive OEM tube assemblies and one-off coded process-pipe spools.
rho.change_evidence: Interviews with estimators, engineers, production managers, quality leaders, and owners at at least fifteen eligible shops, including software stack, data readiness, pilot conversion, customer approval, and realized production use, would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Estimating, drawing search, planning, purchasing, maintenance triage, and quality-exception review can be deployed incrementally and integrated around existing ERP, CAD, and automated equipment. High-mix custom work, weld-procedure qualification, pressure-boundary liability, variable customer drawings, and small-firm implementation capacity slow deployment.

s5.bridge: The Piping Solutions, Tube Bending Technology add-on, and Integrated Manufacturing Concepts transactions are observed transfers in pipe, tube, or closely related fabrication, but they do not measure a five-year hazard for all eligible NAICS 332996 firms. I use them as direct evidence of strategic, private-equity, and individual-buyer liquidity and apply a broad ownership-cycle range. This can overstate transfer rates because announced deals are selected and understate them because small private transactions are often confidential.
caveats: The transactions span adjacent tube fabrication and process-skid activities, so exact six-digit classification may vary.
Published examples are not a population hazard rate and omit failed processes and internal family transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, Calder, regional investment banks, and SBA-backed search acquisitions, matched to a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially change this value.
s5.rationale: Recent acquisitions span strategic capability expansion, PE-backed add-ons, and founder succession, showing multiple buyer channels. Fragmented family ownership and aging founders support supply, while customer concentration, cyclicality, owner relationships, environmental or weld-liability diligence, and uneven management depth reduce completed-transfer probability.

principal_driver: A large custom-fabricator base combines recurring physical demand, observable transfer activity, and document-heavy workflows suitable for AI improvement.
principal_weakness: High-mix physical production, fixed-price project risk, and competitive rebids cap implementation depth and long-run benefit retention.

run_meta.title: Fabricated Pipe and Pipe Fitting Manufacturing

sources.supports: Small and medium manufacturer AI use cases and implementation lessons in predictive maintenance and quality, scrap, throughput, forecasting, data readiness, and incremental pilots.
